replies.author: @flare242
replies.text:
@burntnougat5341 I dunno why people see Cyberpunk that way. Sure, the entire cyberpunk GENRE is a cautionary tale about runaway capitalism, technological advancement outpacing the laws governing such matters, and shady multinational cabals having spooky agendas, but what's that have in common with CD Projekt? Yes, we've learned that the crunch in CD Projekt is known to be brutal, but at least they deliver enjoyable product in the end. 
I was even satisfied with the PC release version. And that wasn't just random contrarianism to bait people. Sure, there were missing features, and the police teleporting two meters behind the player was pretty cringey, but they fixed all of that, eventually. I think that the PC release got shitcanned because of the console release disaster, but i played the vanilla CP right after the release and the worst bugs i've seen was 1 ragdoll clipping through the terrain, stutters, and horrible sharpening artifacts. Along with the police teleporting behind me (as already mentioned), that was all of it. 
I still believe that CD Projekt is one of the VERY RARE AAA devs, who do give a fuck about their player base.
I even suspect that the decision to release on console generation where it had no business being in the first place wasn't made by the devs, because they obviously must have had a solid idea it cannot work. 
I just don't see why everyone is bitching about CP. CD Projekt isn't a cabal of sociopaths targeting addicts with unplayable freemium shit (like basically 95% of android gaming market and old IPs purchased by chinese media conglomerates happen to be. Nor are they (unlike the horrendous media spawned by brainwashed people who stopped thinking for themselves and see anything and everything through the ideological lens) trying to indoctrinate anyone or wave the cautionary finger. CP isn't any kind of an epic self-own as contemporary commie-leaning youth seems to think.
They're just telling a story that ALSO happens to have some antimegacorp messaging (among other ideas and ideologies).

replies.author: @thesonofdormammu5475
replies.text:
IDK have you ever worked with someone who has been in the same job for many many years? All kinds of safety concerns get ignored after a while, very believable that these mid-intelligence scientists were cutting corners. I mean I work part time at a food service place where we have to be very careful about allergens, but the head of the kitchen (who has worked there for 15 years) completely ignores the rules about changing gloves and cleaning surfaces after handling peanuts, eggs and fish. "I don't have time to worry about that and I've never caused an allergic reaction so it's fine." Meanwhile the number one cause of customer injuries for our company isn't slips/falls, it's allergic reactions after eating our food. 
Also keep in mind that they didn't send the best of the best, all of them had some type of criminal/mental health issue history. Sorry but there is NO WAY I would ship out on a 65 year mission, I have kids and I'm not going to miss out on them growing up. Not going to happen. 
Additionally, they lost a good chunk of the crew while catching the specimens. Not hard to imagine that the really smart people were the heads of the exploration crews. This is what is left over. The dregs of the crew if you will.

replies.text:
Well, I can do that same exact thing and write a very enjoyable Alien movie. It's not horror, though. It's comedy. The stoniest stoner on the crew gets facehugger'ed and no one notices until the xeno has escaped into the walls. Things go missing, and everyone's on edge, armed to the teeth. Then an alarm from the canteen. They can't risk their entire food supply, so they arm up and head in. And the Alien is making a grilled cheese and puffing on a blunt. It just stares at them, then hits the doob and passes it. Then the rest of the movie is your typical stoner comedy, just with moments of serious sci-fi. 
The idea, is that it's not mocking the Alien franchise. Just the direction it's movies have been going. It should be canonically accurate. No lore breaks for plot convenience or jokes. This Xeno is just a chill, derpy dude, who's only attack is being annoying. And dumb. 
He doesn't talk, but he understands, and makes extremely human gestures in response. 
Crew: Why aren't you attacking us?
Alien: 🤔... 🤷‍♂️. 🚬🤭.... _🚬👌😮‍💨_

replies.text:
@PBRatLord Um, no. People hate Alien 3 because it's a movie that was butchered by studio interference to the point the director of the film walked out before even finishing the movie. Thematically and visually there are interesting premises but they are all undercooked and half-baked, story elements like the death of main characters are not unpopular because people forgot they are "watching a horror franchise", they are unpopular because of their execution, which was half-assed and a product of last minute re-writes and director changes. The film was getting re-written as it was being shot, had three different directors put their hands on it with the last director, Fincher, leaving the movie in post production and disowning it forever, saying it is not his film and that the experience of making it was hell with him having to work many days without a script at all. 

This absolute disfunction directly comes through in the finished product especially the theatrical version which is downright bad. The film was somewhat salvaged through creative edits in more recent "cuts" that added more than 30 mins of scrapped footage that fill in a lot of the blatant story gaps, for example, Newt and  Higg's death is at least weaved into the visual narrative rather than just explained in a single line of exposition like it was originally. The new revised cuts are  more watchable  but most people who "hate" Alien 3 are talking about the movie as it was originally released. It was a catastrophe for Fox, for the franchise, and for anyone who paid to see it in theaters.

replies.text: Yeah, that's the whole point of a Xenomorph. They are purely brute survivalists that can't be reasoned with, and it's programmed into them. They are super weapons after all, developed by the Engineers to wipe out whole planets. So it doesn't just ignore the original movies, it also ignores literally the most recent lore from the other movies. The whole point is that they can't be reasoned with, because the black goo AI programmed them to do two things: multiply and wipe everything else out, in the most conveniently efficient way possible. That's their entire purpose. They will even forego their own self preservation when attacking, even if they are the only one, which we constantly see in the movies. In the movie where Ripley was on that prison colony, the entire movie only had literally one Xenomorph.  They basically turned the Xenomorphs into a Disney princess pet character. Even in the comics, which differ from the recent movie lore, Xenomorphs actually have a homeworld full of hives, almost a civilization even, with what are essentially city hives.

Yep! I have watched it and slogged on to this conclusion. One, its bad. I expected it to be bad, I hoped it would be average, but its just bad. And that is largely due to the writing once again. I had low expectations watching it, I just hoped it would be better than Romulus, but its not. Its tried to do some new things but its often very predictable. Two, its a typical modern tv show. In that the whole thing looks amazing, cost a lot but is not entertaining to watch, its a chore. The characters are not endearing, they do stupid things, stuff does not make sense. People in responsible positions / work conditions act and talk like teens and not like mature adults with responsibility. The whole thing is another case of style over substance. You watch an episode, get distracted by visuals, explosions, something the looks cool and has potential etc...and go along with it. After watching it and over the next day or so you realise it was all pointless, pathetic, did not  make a lot of sense and has plot holes and things that annoy you. Things where you say - "Wait a minute!? That should not have happened? Why did they do that? Doing the opposite would be more sensible..."
And that is something that applies to so much tv and film today. Its the same feeling I had watching Force Awakens, Last Jedi, Romulus, Last of Us etc...the list is endless. And its why I have avoided things like Gatwa Doctor Who, Echo, Ms Marvel, Loki, Acolyte, Ahsoka, Indiana Jones 5...and so on. That list is also endless. At the start of the summer I had hoped Alien Earth could be watchable, or just average....Instead its been another crap pile among many. And that is the biggest disappointment of all. That and its not surprised me that it should be so....

replies.author: @robonator2945
replies.text:
I've said it before and I'll say it again
The first season of Halo should've had chief as the antagonist. 

The entire first season should've been an Expanse-esque political thriller, following the UNSC trying to crush a rebllion and the rebellion trying to take hold of a planet, skirmishing with each other. Ideally you'd present both sides as empathetic and understandable, but competent and clearly at-odds with eachother. (again, The Expanse does this *_great)_* So, when Chief and his fireteam kick in the door unceremoniously and axe the characters you've been following as just part of the mission, over, and over again, you recognize him immediately as a faceless force of nature, the living, unstoppable, embodiment of the UNSC's will. You want the audience to get to the point where characters can be mid-conversation, and if you hear an explosion and the camera cuts to Chief, you immediately just assume "this is the scene where the character I was just watching dies". I'm talking Game of Thrones level of unceremonious ends, even beyond. It should feel like the plot itself compromises when he enters the room. "Ah well shucks, I had a long, multi-season plan for this character, but Chief is here now, I guess that's that." The audience should begin to think of Chief as a borderline literal grim reaper, unavoidable, uncaring, unwavering. We follow maybe *_one_* character who has seen his efficacy and lived to tell the tale by hiding in a vent or something, but they're the only one who has. (for instance, the Spartans attack a place they've grown up in, their parents tell them to hide in a vent, they watch through the grates above as Chief silently eliminates everyone on the base, things wind down, and as they inch away, blip, motion detected, and instantly chief and the other spartans swing their rifles up without hesitation to blast the vents, with the character only just barely managing to escape because they know every inch of the place and can manuever faster in the vents than the Spartans can follow) Better yet, he's a cunning and ruthless leader, giving his entire team perfect orders on the fly without hesitation. And his entire team listens to his orders and follows them to the letter with equally little hesitation.

Except, throughout the season we also know both sides are losing ships, outposts, etc. at a high rate. Both sides attribute this to the actions of the other, the UNSC assuming the rebels destroyed their base, infiltrated their ships, etc. and the rebels assuming the inverse. Except we're watching both sides, we can realize things aren't lining up; they *_haven't_* been destroying each other's ships, so who, or what, has? (maybe even have short dialog between some of the other spartans asking things like "how the hell are these guys taking us down? They havent even managed to scratch us, and they managed to kill Martin?!" while Chief silently continues cleaning his rifle, training, etc.)

Culminating in the season finale where it all comes to a head, with the last bastion of the rebellion being stormed by the UNSC, the Spartans are waiting for next orders on what to do with the disarmed prisoners, and they don't come... it's static. Try again... static. Do you copy? ... static. Then some of the half-busted screens at the base start going off, incoming ships have been detected, and they aren't UNSC. Then at the *_veeeeery_* tail end of the season you have Chief finally, for the first time in the entire show, visibly hesitate and pause to think before giving his order. (dear god, no facecam, just a slow zoom dead-on his helmet) He tells his team to uncuff the prisoners and give them back their weapons, because he's already realized that whoever is outside are the ones responsible for their allies' deaths, and they'll need all the help they can get.

So you spend the entire season building up Chief as who he should be, an unstoppable force of nature; competence given form. Then, at the very last moment of the show, you have him side not with the UNSC or the rebellion, but with humanity, against the covenant. This not only characterizes him how he should be, not only makes him a massive badass, and not only gives him actual character depth (as someone theoretically pro-human and duty-bound, but borderline mechanical and uncaring) BUT it's actually in-line with the lore, as the Spartans were originally created as basically a peacekeeping force and only became the military spearhead because "well, we've already been training up these supersoldiers, might as well use 'em for something better than just putting down a few rabblerousers". And to top it all off? It's the cliffyist cliffhanger to ever hang off a cliff.

The issue is trying to make chief the protagonist; he's not, he can't be. He is an intentionally bland character. He was originally created as a faceless, voiceless self-insert. While there can be some depth to that sort of shallowness (again, you can really lean into the idea of him as this 'saviour of humanity' who is so disconnected from humanity some people genuinely don't believe he even *_is_* a human and think he's an experimental combat robot or something) it just can't work as the 'protagonist' in the traditional sense. Chief is a foil, not the protagonist. If you try to make him the protagonist you will fail. Less Holden, more Amos. He *_can_* develop, but you can't make a show around it because he needs to develop so slowly and needs to be so impersonal that it's just not feasible. S1 is him as the force of nature, S2-3 is the Halo 4 "soldiers aren't machines" arc where you see people more explicitly challenging him about his nature as he himself begins to question it, then only at Season 4-5 do you start even *_thinking_* about going with the true rogue-agent Chief we get glimpses of in Halo 5.

author: @PABaines
text:
The first Alien was a masterpiece. The slowly building dread was almost suffocating. When we did finally see the xenomorph, it was with a memorable jump scare. The second movie carried that dread into an action format and delivered so many unforgettable moments and quotable lines.

What worked was that they were about a creature completely alien to our understanding. Even the opening credits hint at this, with the word "ALIEN" slowly emerging. Everything about the creature is alien to us and the first movie ended with us still knowing very little about it. The second film kept the mystery going and showed how dangerous they are.

I doubt they'll ever be able to match the sheer "alienness" of the first two movies. They worked because the monsters stayed in the shadows and allowed our imaginations to fill in the gaps. It seems like every sequel since then has brought the creature more and more out of the shadows, making them less scary. In Alien Earth, the sheep is creepier than the xenomorph!

I've enjoyed every Alien movie so far and am quite enjoying Alien Earth for what it is. but I am not expecting anything to come close to brilliance of the first two.

author: @TheBigDScoop
text:
I really dislike this show, largely due to its horrendous writing, but what bothers me most is the design of the Alien itself. The other creatures range from mediocre to decent, but the Xenomorph — the supposed star of the series — looks consistently awful in every scene. The suit appears rubbery, oddly proportioned, and the more it’s shown in brightly lit environments, the worse it looks. By contrast, the 1979 film’s suit was far more convincing, with careful framing in shadow and close-ups that masked its limitations.

On a smaller note, I also found it laughable that when the science vessel crashed on Earth, all of the equipment, tools, and even corpses somehow stayed neatly in place — as if everything aboard the ship had been magnetized.

replies.author: @mikepatton8691
replies.text: I think the groups of scientists in the lab and the soldiers that tried to capture Wendy and friends in the jungle would disagree on the xenomorph losing it's fearsome reputation. Let's not forget that most xenomorphs are drones and they are likely to be predisposed to following orders of a queen or an alpha. Wendy having a fully synthetic body takes her off the list of potential food or a potential host, and her not making any threatening moves towards the xenomorph when it was born makes her not a threat. Wendy not being any of those things opens up a possibility that the xenomorph could imprint on her when it was born, especially because she was able to communicate with it in some way immediately after it emerged. I think the show has handled the whole thing in a good way and I actually really like the show and I'm hoping for a second season. I also have to mention the real star of the show, T. Ocellus, the eye octopus. I really like that this show has given us some new creatures to watch besides the xenomorph. Obviously people like what they like and not everyone is going to like the same thing but I'm really glad we got this show.

author: @frostydog860
text:
I'm baffled by a number of things in this show...

1. With a trillion dollar company, how do they NOT have more staff on hand who are actively watching the monitors?? You'd expect billion-dollar prototypes to be heavily monitored with multiple redundancies in place. It's like watching Jurassic Park where there's a single point of failure from an IT person.

2. The android Kirsch has a completely confusing motivation. What is his objective? Even if he's just a curious android, he's inherently a computer who must abide by his programming (i.e., should be putting into place strong security protocols with multiple redundancies for each of the test subjects).

3. Again on the monitoring side... nobody besides Kirsch is monitoring the fact that Slightly is just talking to himself?

4. These are essentially kids in adult bodies. They need love and attention--otherwise this is just Homelander all over again. You could say that profit motive reduces these kids to just "products", but even a business person knows that they have to maintain their "products" in order to sell them.

EDIT:

I thought of more... 

There's also the issue of the guy who betrayed the crew that set off all of the pieces in the first place. There's no way that a ship that's carrying a highly valuable cargo like that isn't monitoring the video calls of its crew. MTHR would have informed another member of the crew if that negotiation was happening with a competitor, and dealt with the guy right away.

author: @ehsimeserve8324
text:
It's the repetitive, unimaginative, very american cliché of telling you that the monster used to be good and it became a monster only because it suffered, or it was abused, or it simply is misunderstood. Which also sends this horrible message that you are supposed to empathise with a monster because at some point it might have actually been a victim. It's the banalization of evil.

Coppola's Dracula, Darth Vader, Harvey Dent, Magneto, Cruella, Maleficent, Scar in Mufasa, Poison Ivy, Thanos, Starro in The suicide squad, Boba Fett, Alien's xenomorph, even the machines in Animatrix!!! The list is endless really, and we are always supposed to say "oh you see, they were evil but they suffered, i get it, they are the real victims".
It's so stupid and so terribly banal.

replies.author: @bernardkelly1067
replies.text: I think the Darth Vader one is unfair (discounting some of the disappointing elements of the prequels re his character beats). He was a good guy becomes bad, becomes good again briefly and dies. We're never meant to think his actions were justifiable i.e. mass murder. My reading of Darth Vader is that the lack of acceptance and misunderstanding created a scenario of resentment where the truly irredeemable villain took advantage of him and dragged him into evil. But has a hopeful note in that there can be modicum of redemption for the repentent. Lucas wouldn't have got away with Luke and Anakin going away on womp rat hunting trips on Tatooine. Vader's arc is not comparable with Noah Hawley's reimagined misunderstood puppy dog xenomorph. The show was losing me but that tipped me over the edge. The only way the show can redeem itself if it shows that the alien is so cunning that it's using Wendy as a means to get off the island. Then it will kill her for being so dumb and get a puppy pregnant with a xenomorph as a tongue in cheek joke. The lore even says they're just killing machines. Anyway go away now.

​@ChimpingBulldog it's a channel about movies, and this is a very common trait of modern hollywood storytelling, so i listed movies. Why would i have to give examples taken from literature, if i am openly criticising storytelling of modern films? I also wrote it's an AMERICAN cliché and you ask me why i don't include Shakespeare and Milton? 😂

By the way, the first example i can think of in Shakespeare is Iago in Othello, and i cannot recall any intent by the author to make Iago likeable, to make you empathise with him or to tell us "he used to be a good guy". He is a vicious piece of trash and you are perfectly entitled to find him repulsive and wicked. He is, becoming a destructive bastard over a non given promotion.

replies.author: @ehsimeserve8324
replies.text:
​@GigaWhatt0 i disagree completely with your last phrase. Because "I hate to break it to you" but A LOT of people in the world are born twisted and evil. A lot.
I cannot even count the number of murderers, serial killers or perverted criminals who were born into normal environments, had suffered no abuse or violence whatsoever, and from a very young age started behaving like monsters anyway. You can meet people like that in your life, they exist and they are disturbing.

Having something that causes you to unleash your evil is one thing, assuming that before that you were nice and just a regular guy, or even full of love, is the most used cliché in Hollywood, and that's exactly what i am saying. Seeing it over and over in movies and series is repetitive and unimaginative. Can't they invent something different than "he is bad because he was wronged when he was good"?

One of the coolest things in movies, and one of the scariest in real life, is not knowing everything about, or not understanding evil.
True evil is scary, and a lot of times it has no explanation.

replies.author: @ehsimeserve8324

​@bernardkelly1067 you are probably right about Dart Vader, i just listed all of those i could quickly think of, and i am sure there can be some that is a bit more complex than others, but the point is: even if in some case it works well, if some of those origin stories work in creating interest (most of them don't, for me), it's a format that's been used so much that became tiring and boring.
And they try to rehabilitate some character that for me is laughable for how unacceptable it is, like Cruella or the machines in Animatrix. Those machines who harvest human beings were victims, they were just trying to do the right thing, but they were mistreated so they went violent.

It hurts me to even think someone could come up with this idea 😂

replies.author: @ehsimeserve8324
replies.text:
​@yhp99 but that is the thing, of course there is nothing wrong with complexity. What i am saying is that there is no complexity at all in this kind of idea. "We need to create an origin story for the evil villain. Let's make it a good guy who turned evil for some reason!" 
It's the opposite of complexity for me, it's the easiest choice.

replies.author: @yhp99
replies.text: @ehsimeserve8324 I think a good way to think about it is to discard the specific characters or creatures that we define as evil. I dont think they are the main characters here. Rather they are the tools we use to enhance our understanding of good and evil. Entertainment using the idea of pure evil and pure good is no longer a realistic method for many people because reality is that there are so many areas of grey. To many people it clearly muddies the waters.  So the new assignment is to understand the grey rather than the black and white.

replies.author: @yhp99
replies.text: @ehsimeserve8324 I think it doesnt have to be a "good guy" turning evil. Rather it can be anyone.  That is the complexity.  No particular path. Rather there are many paths to evil and many paths to good.  And sometimes good can lead to evil and visa versa.  The complexity of reality is worth exploring even if it means having to endure some banality in some interpretations along the way

replies.author: @ehsimeserve8324
replies.text:
​@yhp99 fair enough, i think pointing out that it could be anyone and that there are many paths to evil or good, are both very valid points. I agree on this.
Still, there are certain occasions where this doesn't apply, or where it doesn't really work in my opinion. The best example is with creatures, like the xenomorph being treated as a dog, or the orc not wanting to go to war because of his son and his partner in The Rings of Power.
It takes away mistery, danger, and appeal from the villain. And in these cases, it humanises two beings that should incarnate evil and the opposite of humanity.

replies.author: @yhp99
replies.text: @ehsimeserve8324 Youre very open minded to acknowledge the many paths theory to evil and good. Then I would also ask you to keep an open mind to the (unfortunate)possibility that we "need" something to call evil...rather than actually trying to identify evil in an empirical sense.  Our emotions dictating what evil is and then drowning ourselves in that notion because it makes us feel "good".  I think that was the way things were because we were new to it or we were young.  But now its no longer necessary nor reasonable.  There is enough grey in this complex world that if we want any serious discussion or storytelling. we need origins of the things that make us instinctually cringe.  That is a step forward imo
